title: Health and the Body
description: Talk about your health like a B1 speaker: describe symptoms with doler (me duele/duelen) and tener fiebre/tos/gripe, handle a visit to el médico and la farmacia (la receta, las pastillas, el jarabe, la cita), and give advice with the subjunctive and conditional (Te recomiendo que descanses; Deberías ir al médico). Plus the classic false friends estar constipado and embarazada.

cards.front: Me duele la cabeza. / Me duelen los pies.
cards.back: to hurt — doler works like gustar (the body part is the subject)
cards.notes: *Doler* behaves like *gustar*: the body part is the grammatical subject, so the verb agrees with it. **Singular** part → *duele* (*me duele la cabeza*); **plural** parts → *duelen* (*me duelen los pies*). The person who hurts is an indirect-object pronoun: *me, te, le, nos, os, les*.

cards.back: use the definite article, not the possessive, with body parts
cards.notes: With body parts Spanish uses the **definite article** (*la, el, los, las*), not the possessive. Say *me duele **la** cabeza*, *me lavo **las** manos* — never *me duele **mi** cabeza*. The pronoun (*me*) already tells you whose body it is.

cards.front: tener fiebre, tos, gripe, un resfriado
cards.back: to have a fever, a cough, the flu, a cold
cards.notes: Common ailments use **tener** + noun, no article for some: *tener **fiebre*** (a fever), *tener **tos*** (a cough), *tener **gripe*** (the flu), *tener **un resfriado*** (a cold). Note: *gripe* and *resfriado* are everyday words for flu and cold.

cards.back: to be dizzy, to feel weak
cards.notes: Temporary states use **estar** or **sentirse**: *estar **mareado/a*** (dizzy/light-headed), *estar **cansado/a*** (tired), *sentirse **débil*** (to feel weak). These agree in gender: *ella está mareada*.

cards.back: FALSE FRIEND: to have a cold / be congested — NOT 'constipated'
cards.notes: **False friend!** *Estar constipado* means *to have a cold / a stuffy nose*, not *constipated*. If you really mean *constipated*, say *estar **estreñido/a***. So *Estoy constipado* = *I have a cold*, while *Estoy estreñido* = *I'm constipated*.

**False friend!** *Embarazada* means *pregnant*, not *embarrassed*. For *embarrassed* use *avergonzado/a* or *me da vergüenza*. So *Está embarazada* = *She's pregnant*, while *Estoy avergonzado* = *I'm embarrassed*.

cards.front: el síntoma, el diagnóstico, la cita, la urgencia
cards.notes: At the doctor's: *el **síntoma*** (symptom — masculine despite the -a), *el **diagnóstico*** (diagnosis), *pedir una **cita*** (to make an appointment), *ir a **urgencias*** (to go to A&E / the emergency room).

cards.front: la receta, las pastillas, el jarabe
cards.notes: At the pharmacy: *la **receta*** (prescription — also 'recipe'!), *las **pastillas*** (pills/tablets), *el **jarabe*** (syrup, e.g. for a cough), *el **seguro médico*** (health insurance). *Tomar las pastillas* = to take the pills.

cards.back: advice verbs (recomendar, aconsejar) take the subjunctive
cards.notes: Verbs of advice and recommendation trigger the **subjunctive** after *que*: *Te recomiendo que **descanses*** (I recommend that you rest), *Te aconsejo que **bebas** agua*, *El médico quiere que **tomes** las pastillas*. English uses an infinitive here, so don't say *Te recomiendo que descansas*.

cards.back: soften advice with the conditional: deberías + infinitive
cards.notes: The conditional **deberías** (you should) gives gentle advice and is followed by the **infinitive**: *Deberías ir al médico*, *Deberías descansar más*. Compare *podrías* (you could). This is softer than the command *Ve al médico*.

cards.back: to lead a healthy life, to take care of oneself
cards.notes: Healthy-lifestyle phrases: *llevar una **vida sana*** (to lead a healthy life), *hacer **ejercicio*** (to exercise), *cuidarse* (to look after oneself), *comer **sano*** (to eat healthily). *¡Cuídate!* = Take care!

cards.front: Es importante que tomes las pastillas.
cards.back: impersonal expressions of advice also take the subjunctive
cards.notes: Impersonal expressions like *Es importante que...*, *Es necesario que...*, *Es mejor que...* trigger the **subjunctive**: *Es importante que **tomes** las pastillas*, *Es mejor que **vayas** al médico*. Without a specific subject, use the infinitive: *Es importante descansar*.

steps.title: Saying what hurts: doler like gustar
steps.body:
# Health and the Body

At B1 you can do more than name body parts — you can describe how you feel, talk to a doctor, and give advice.

## doler works like gustar

To say something hurts, use **doler**, and treat it exactly like *gustar*: the **body part is the subject**, and the verb agrees with it.

| | singular part | plural parts |
|---|---|---|
| me | me **duele** la cabeza | me **duelen** los pies |
| te | te **duele** la espalda | te **duelen** las piernas |
| le | le **duele** el estómago | le **duelen** las muelas |

The person is an **indirect-object pronoun** (*me, te, le, nos, os, les*):

- *Me duele **la** cabeza.* — My head hurts.
- *Me duelen **las** piernas.* — My legs hurt.

## Use the article, not the possessive

With body parts Spanish uses the **definite article**, not *mi/tu*:

- ✓ *Me duele **la** cabeza.*  ✗ *Me duele **mi** cabeza.*

The pronoun *me* already shows whose head it is.

steps.title: Symptoms, the doctor and the pharmacy
steps.body:
## Describing symptoms

Most ailments use **tener** + a noun:

- *Tengo **fiebre**.* — I have a fever.
- *Tengo **tos**.* — I have a cough.
- *Tengo **gripe** / un **resfriado**.* — I have the flu / a cold.

Temporary states use **estar** or **sentirse**:

- *Estoy **mareado/a**.* — I'm dizzy.
- *Me siento **débil**.* — I feel weak.

## At the doctor and the pharmacy

- *Pedir una **cita*** — to make an appointment
- *Los **síntomas**, el **diagnóstico*** — symptoms, the diagnosis
- *La **receta**, las **pastillas**, el **jarabe*** — prescription, pills, syrup
- *Ir a **urgencias**, el **seguro médico*** — to go to A&E, health insurance

## Two famous false friends

> **estar constipado** does **not** mean *constipated* — it means *to have a cold / a stuffy nose*. *Constipated* = **estreñido/a**.
>
> **embarazada** does **not** mean *embarrassed* — it means *pregnant*. *Embarrassed* = **avergonzado/a**.

steps.title: Giving advice: subjunctive and conditional
steps.body:
## Advice with the subjunctive

Verbs of advice/recommendation take **que + subjunctive** when there's a new subject:

- *Te **recomiendo** que **descanses**.* — I recommend (that) you rest.
- *Te **aconsejo** que **bebas** mucha agua.*
- *El médico **quiere** que **tomes** las pastillas.*

Impersonal expressions do the same:

- *Es **importante** que **tomes** las pastillas.*
- *Es **mejor** que **vayas** al médico.*

> Watch out: English uses an infinitive (*I recommend you to rest*), but Spanish needs *que* + subjunctive. Don't say *Te recomiendo que descansas*.

## Softer advice with the conditional

**Deberías** (you should) + **infinitive** gives gentle advice:

- *Deberías **ir** al médico.*
- *Deberías **descansar** más.*  ·  *Podrías **tomar** un jarabe.*

## A healthy lifestyle

- *llevar una **vida sana*** — to lead a healthy life
- *hacer **ejercicio**, comer **sano**, **cuidarse*** — to exercise, eat healthily, take care of yourself
- *¡**Cuídate**!* — Take care!

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish health word with its English meaning.
Translate: *My legs hurt.* (doler agrees with the plural body part; use the article)
exercise.hint: Plural body part → duelen; use 'las', not 'mis'.

steps.title: False friend: I have a cold

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I have a cold.* (use the false friend constipado — it means congested, not 'constipated')
exercise.hint: estar constipado = to have a cold; NOT estreñido (= constipated).

exercise.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'She is pregnant'? (mind the false friend)
Fill in the correct form of doler for a single body part (la cabeza).
exercise.sentence: Me ___ la cabeza desde esta mañana.
exercise.hint: One singular part (la cabeza) → duele; note 'la', not 'mi'.

steps.title: Build: I recommend that you rest

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *I recommend that you rest.* (recomendar que + subjunctive)
Fill in the present subjunctive of tomar (tú) after 'Es importante que'.
exercise.sentence: Es importante que ___ las pastillas tres veces al día.
exercise.hint: Es importante que + subjunctive; tomar → tomes (tú).

exercise.prompt: Translate: *You should go to the doctor.* (deberías + infinitive)
exercise.hint: deberías + infinitive (no subjunctive here): ir al médico.

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish phrase with its English meaning.

pairs.right: I recommend that you drink water.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *The doctor recommends that you rest.* (recomendar que + subjunctive, tú)
distractors: El médico recomienda que descanses mi.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the verb used with symptoms like fiebre.
exercise.sentence: No me encuentro bien: ___ fiebre y tos.
exercise.hint: Symptoms use tener: yo → tengo fiebre.
